title: Neural Network Structure Optimization by Simulated Annealing

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## NETWORK PRUNING OPTIMIZATION BY SIMULATED ANNEALING ALGORITHM

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

One critical problem of large neural networks is over-parameterization with a large number of weight parameters. This becomes an obstacle to implement networks in edge devices as well as limiting the development of industrial applications by engineers for machine learning problems. Plenty of papers have shown that the redundant branches can be erased strategically in a fully connected network. In this work, we reduce network complexity by pruning and structure optimization. We propose to do network optimization by Simulated Annealing, a heuristic based non-convex optimization method which can potentially solve this NP-hard problem and find the global minimum for a given percentage of branch pruning given sufficient amount of time. Our results have shown that Simulated Annealing can significantly reduce the complexity of a fully connected neural network with only limited loss of performance.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

The successful development of the artificial intelligence methods fueled by deep-learning has made important impact in multiple industries. Elaborate AI algorithm can empower electronic devices to make smart decisions or foresighted predictions. However, the outstanding outcomes come with their own cost, which is the computational complexity in the case of deep-learning methods. In order to obtain a model that can accurately predict the results corresponding to the input data, a neural network should go through initialization, back propagation, gradients update, and inference process step by step. When the deep learning structure is more complicated, there are more parameters and operations involving in these 4 steps. Network compression technique is a relatively new area necessitated by the booming development of deep neural network and increasing computational complexity of machine learning methods. Attracted by the extraordinary performance of recently proposed neural network architectures, there are increasingly more demands on edge devices which possess only limited computational resources. Therefore, various network compression techniques have been tried and studied during these years. The recent mainstream strategies include quantization (Wang et al., 2019), decomposition (Zhang et al., 2015), knowledge distillation (Hinton et al., 2014), and the pruning methods, which is considered as the most effective approach to compress a neural network (Liu et al., 2019b) (Lin et al., 2020a). Among those 4 computational steps of deep learners mentioned above, back propagation and gradients update make up the major part that consumes the most of computing resources. One approach by pruning those gradients that are smaller than a threshold (Ye et al., 2020), significantly reduces the usage of hardware resources and accelerates the whole training procedure. However, the trained model would still have the same parameter size after undergoing the pruned training procedure since the actual weight parameters are not eliminated from the network from computational perspective. Thus, this approach is only helpful for training a network on a powerful computer. In order to actually reduce the total size of a model while inferencing in a mobile electronic device, various research found in the survey (Deng et al., 2020) focus on pruning the weight parameters directly instead of the gradients. These pruning techniques can be element-wise, vector-wise, or block-wise, which can correspond to unstructured or structured pruning. Behind each type of pruning technique, there are multiple criteria being setup differently. One innovative criteria is the combination of generative adversarial network (GAN) and parameters selection (Madaan et al., 2020). By evaluating the similarity of the feature maps before and after pruning process, the importance of certain parameters can be defined.

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Except for evaluating the feature map with a designed criteria setup in GAN, the other group of pruning strategies follow heuristic optimization algorithms, where the network is considered as an architecture fine-tuning process. Since a neural network is one type of network composed of both nodes and edges, it would be intuitive to set up the optimization problem in terms of the edge weights and the structure of the network and the pruning strategy. ABC optimization technique is an example that demonstrates the application of a heuristic algorithm on channel-wise network pruning (Lin et al., 2020b). Based on the hierarchical characteristic of the neural network structure, pruning techniques can further be split into both structured and unstructured pruning categories. When a neural network is simulated on computer, the major part of computations would be carried out in matrix operations including addition, multiplication, and dot product. Therefore, in the case of unstructured pruning, the edges or nodes are not truly being eliminated. Instead, an extra mask matrix is designed to simulate the pruning process. Even though the mask matrix can bring up high flexibility in adjusting the network configuration, the model size cannot be reduced significantly since the pruned parameters should still be conserved in the matrix as zero entries. On the contrary, the structured pruning strategies sacrifice some optimality to ensure dimension reduction and avoid sparse but high dimensional matrices. The most representative cases for both unstructured and structured pruning are edge-level and node-level pruning strategies. if one edge of a network is considered as the minimum unit to reduce configuration, it would not affect the existance of all other elements in the network. However, if one node is the minimum unit to be reduce a network, all other edges connected to the selected node will also need to be erased.

In choosing pruning strategies, the main factor is to determine which part of the minimum unit (edges, nodes, ... etc) is more important than the others. The existing pruning approaches do not consider the optimality of the resulting network structure in any means. The optimality under question has two aspects to it: the branch weights and the branch connections. In this work, we aim to optimize the branch connections with the constraint of pruning percentage, That is, we question the optimality of the structure obtained by pruning. In this paper, we augment the network pruning operation with a structure optimization algorithm. For this task we propose simulated annealing (SA) algorithm. By considering the permutation of the pruned configurations as a finite Markov chain, the best remaining configuration can be cleverly searched by Simulated Annealing. Different from the other pruning approaches which defined additional fancy objective function and constraints, our approach directly takes the loss function of a neural network as an objective function. The optimized network is guaranteed to be the best permutated configuration under the fixed parameters. In addition, since the loss function is used to both configuration optimization and parameters update with back propagation, the optimal configuration and the remaining parameters can further be trained to fine-tune the parameters seamlessly.

## 2 RELATED WORKS

With the increasing applications of deep learning, there are more and more demands for the portability of the DNNs and two lines of research evolved over the years to reduce the computational and storage requirements of DNNs. The first line of research focused on reducing the storage requirements of DNNs; this is achieved by formulating the problem as source coding or data compression problem and making use of existing quantization methods. Examples of this approach include: low bit quantization (Hubara et al., 2018), multi-level quantization (Xu et al., 2018) and clustering based quantization (Choi et al., 2017). More elaborate source coding schemes are also employed such as Huffman encoding in (Han et al., 2016) and trellis coded quantization (Haase et al., 2020) and entropy based coding (Wiedemann et al., 2020).

The other line of approach aims at reducing the number of parameters in the neural network by making it more sparse which is achieved by pruning. The pruning approach was motivated by the very early (pre-deeplearning) works of LeCun et al (LeCun et al., 1989) and Hassibi and Stork (Hassibi & Stork, 1992), which showed that neural networks can still work well when many edges or nodes are pruned.

## 2.1 NETWORK PRUNING TECHNIQUES

As exemplified in _deep compression_ (Han et al., 2016), one simple yet effective pruning criteria is to select those weights with absolute magnitude smaller than a threshold. The process is executed

based on a pretrained network. Once the small weights are pruned, the remaining weights will be fine-tuned by training with the dataset again. Various pruning algorithms differ in their choice of where they apply the threshold such as the output of activation function or the gradient of each weight parameter or the magnitude of the weights.

It has also been reported in the deep compression approach (Han et al., 2016) that pruning the network gradually by picking up only a small fraction of total weights parameters at one time can help to maintain the performance of the pruned network. However, the sparse network still occupies a lot of space in a computer because the pruned weights are all represented by zeros. In order to truely eliminate the parameters from the network, (Chen et al., 2020) proposed tight compression which converts the resulting large sparse matrix after pruning to a smaller but dense matrix by moving nonzero members to matrix locations with zeros. These moves are not done in a deterministic manner but instead via stochastic heuristic algorithm namely simulated annealing.

## 2.2 NEURAL ARCHITECTURE SEARCH (NAS)

Despite the conceptual simplicity and ease of application of pruning, it is a valid question to ask whether the resulting sparse networks are optimal. That is, do the resulting networks structures (with the weights assigned after tuning) provide globally optimal results for given data sets. The straight answer to this question is that given the pruned structures, the tuned weights provide local optimals but this observation tells us nothing about the optimality of the structures. This issue has rarely been discussed in the pruning literature. There are some work, however, which consider neural architecture search mostly out of the context of pruning.

The researchers have considered the structure of a DNN as composed of operational blocks. These blocks include convolution, activation functions, normalization functions, regularization functions, and so many. According to the various combinations of these blocks, different structures of DNN can be constructed. The constructing process can be designed to progress while the model is being trained. By selectively searching all possible queued operations, the optimal configuration can also be determined. When there are more types of operations, or the operation queue is getting longer, the total number of possible states will grow exponentially. This could be a severe problem in neural architecture search especially when it follows grid-search strategy (Zoph & Le, 2017). More effective ways to search for the optimal combination of blocks and permutation of active branches were also proposed by Zoph (Zoph et al., 2018), which stacks the basic blocks sequentially and gradually instead of directly finding the optimal whole network architecture. One probabilistic model-based dynamic optimization method tried to implement a penalty term to the objective function so that the gradients can lead to a better convergence based on a generic framework, which determines a network structure by a vector (Saito & Shirakawa, 2019) (Shirakawa et al., 2018). The other perspective of structure search is to construct a computational graph by including all potential operational blocks (Liu et al., 2019a) (Pham et al., 2018). These approaches all focused on the macro scale of structure optimization without considering the importance of each parameters contained in an operation. Even though the optimal combination of blocks are determined, these structures still may have redundant parameters for weights pruning.

Optimization of structure in micro scale avoiding redundant branches seems to be rare in the literature. One exception is (Nowakowski et al., 2018) which utilizes genetic algorithms for the structure optimization task. Our proposed methodology, is similar in spirit to this work, and is detailed in the next section.

## 3 NETWORK PRUNING WITH SIMULATED ANNEALINGS

In this work, we propose a heuristic non-convex optimization algorithm, namely Simulated annealing (SA) for structure optimization of partially connected neural networks after pruning. The choice of Simulated Annealing has been motivated by the success of the algorithm in various problems involving network/graph structures with large number of configurations and complicated cost surfaces with various local minima (Kuruoglu & Arndt, 2017), (Kuruoglu & Ayanoglu, 1993), (Liu et al., 2021).
SA is motivated by the annealing process in solid state physics, which aims to place the electrons in a solid at their lowest energy states achieving lowest possible energy configuration (Metropolis

3

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

et al., 1953). Similar to typical discrete optimization problems, SA is given a finite set containing all possible configurations _C_ from a fully connected neural network _N_ ( _·_ ) and a loss function _L_ ( _·_ ), and looks for **c** _[∗] ∈C_ such that _L_ ( **c** _[∗]_ ) is minimized. The loss function defined in the optimization problem of neural network is the energy function defined in solid state physics. Different from the panaroma of gradient descent algorithms, which are very likely to be trapped in a local optima, Simulated Annealing algorithm determines the global optima by doing a search in the solution space following a Markov chain random walk.

Simulated Annealing is a general algorithm which can be applied to various problems, which is documented by its success in vastly different application areas; however as in any general algorithm, it requires the design of its components by the user according to the application.
The success of SA depends on careful design of three crucial mechanisms: state neighbourhood structure selection, acceptance-rejection criteria of proposals and the cooling schedule.

## 3.1 CHOICE OF STATE NEIGHBORHOOD STRUCTURE

The choice of neighborhood structure dictates the possible moves the random walk can make; hence it affects significantly convergence rate. A too conservative neighbourhood structure would make the exploration of the solution space very slow while a too liberal choice may make the random walk jump over important minima and will not learn enough turning it into a blind random search algorithm on all possible configurations. However, a conservative or liberal neighbourhood structure does not always provide the same level of disadvantage or advantage. At the beginning of annealing process, big changes between neighborhoods can be helpful for a quick (and coarse) search of the solution space. When a potentially interesting part of the solution space has been reached, smaller moves in a conservatively defined neighbourhood would be more beneficial there are more space being looked around, small changes turn out to be more beneficial to capture the global optimum. On the other hand starting with too conservative neighborhoods would have made the algorithm stuck in local minima nearby the starting configuration.

For the network structure optimization problem, we define the states in the solution space as the configuration of branches connecting nodes in one layer to the next one. Equivalently, we consider a mask matrix _Ml_ which is filled with 1’s at locations ( _i, j_ ) where there is a surviving branch between the nodes _i_ and _j_ after pruning and with 0’s at locations ( _i, j_ ) where there are no surviving branches.

The neighbouring states are defined to be new configurations obtained by moving one (or more) branch(es) originally at location ( _i, j_ ) to ( _i, k_ ). Equivalently, can be visualised as the move of one 1 from matrix location ( _i, j_ ) to another position on the same row ( _i, k_ ). The choice of a single branch replacement corresponds to a conservative neighbourhood structure. It can be made more liberal by considering more than one replacements.

## 3.2 ACCEPTANCE-REJECTION RATIO

Simulated Annealing algorithm can jump out of local optimum is since it occasionally accepts a new state that increases the outcome of the loss function _L_ ( _·_ ) . This mechanism reflects an analogy with the electron dynamics in solid state physics, when the electron has enough thermal energy it can jump over barriers and end up in higher energy states and hence can avoid some local energy minima. As in the case of electron dynamics, SA adopts a Boltzmann distribution to decide accepting or rejecting a move or a step of the random walk:

At every move, _−_ ∆ _L_ ( _·_ ) = _L_ ( _M_ ) _−L_ ( _−M[′]_ ) is calculated, if it is larger than 0, the new state is accepted, otherwise a uniform number is generated between [0 _,_ 1) and compared with _Pb_ . If _Pb_ greater it is accepted, otherwise it is rejected.

## 3.3 CONVERGENCE

An important question is whether this accept/reject scheme and the choice of neighbourhood structure ensure convergence. The answer to this question is well known in the simulated annealing and Markov chain theory literature (van Laarhoven & Aarts, 1987).

A (finite-state) Markov chain converges to a unique stationary distribution only if it has two fundamental properties: aperiodicity and irreducibility or equivalently ergodicity. These properties are not easy to check generally and therefore a stronger property is used, namely detailed balance.

For our problem, the detailed balance condition can be expressed as:

_P_ **c** indicates the probability that the configuration is sampled under the representation of mask matrix _**M**_ , and _**M**[′]_ indicates the first order neighbor configuration of the Markov chain after _**M**_ .

It can be shown that the accept/reject mechanism using the Boltzmann function in Equation (1) ensures that the detailed balance condition and therefore converges to the unique stationary distribution (van Laarhoven & Aarts, 1987). This property is shared with the Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithm which aims to obtain the posterior distribution of model parameters. Both algorithms construct Metropolis loops via random walks over a Markov chains satisfying the detailed balance condition. The difference is that Simulated Annealing algorithm employs several Metropolis loops with decreasing temperature parameter _T_ in Equation 1. Hence, posterior distribution approximated by every Metropolis loop gets more and more peaked at the maximum of the stationary distribution. In this way, the parameter values that maximize the stationary distribution of the Markov chains is obtained.

## 3.4 COOLING SCHEME AND HYPERPARAMETERS

Another design issue affecting the convergence rate of SA is the cooling scheme. It is known that the convergence is guaranteed in the case of logaritmic cooling, however this requires infinitely slow cooling. Instead, most users prefer geometric cooling

The important parameters to be set are the initial temperature ( _Tinit_ ), the cooling rate ( _η_ ) between Metrolopis loops, and the Metropolis loop length ( _MLL_ ). The temperature decrease controls the acceptance-rejection probability for a new state that has higher loss value. If the temperature is high, the worse states are more likely to be accepted. As the temperature is reduced increasingly, the worse states are more likely to be rejected. The decreasing rate is the main factor responsible for handling the speed of the whole annealing process. However, a faster annealing process does not guarantee the convergence of a optimization problem. The objective function can be minimized only when the decreasing speed is slow enough. In our work, we setup the initial _T_ as 10 and _η_ as 0.98. The third parameter _MLL_ should be large enough for the Markov chain to converge to the stationary distribution and small enough not lose time unnecessarily beyond convergence.

## 3.5 HEURISTIC ATTENTION MECHANISM

Attention mechanism aims at finding the importance of elements contained in a network. If the element is more important than the others, its weighted factor, which is represented as a number, will be larger. Generally speaking, the element can be anything except for weight parameters. For example, the elements defined in SqueezeNet (Hu et al., 2018) are the channels in 2D convolutional operation. By including the weighted factors to multiply by each channel of the feature maps in the forward pass, back propagation can be applied to learn the importance of each element. Different from the typical learning method to achieve attention mechanism, Simulated Annealing finds the important elements by trial-and-error with Boltzmann probability. However, unlike the continuous values given from the derivatives using back propagation, the weighted factors computed by our approach would be discrete numbers _{_ 0 _,_ 1 _}_ where 0 and 1 indicate that the elements are ignorable and crucial respectively. These two types of values are actually the fundamental elements composing the mask matrix ( _M_ ). Being a new incorporation between SA and network pruning, we call our method as the heuristic attention mechanism. The details are described in **Algorithm 1** where a network takes a dataset _T_ and a set of mask matrices as inputs. The _c_ marked in the bracket indicates the _c-th_ type of configuration represented by _M_ .

## 3.6 PERMUTATION AFTER EDGES PRUNING

According to **Algorithm 1** , the mechanism starts by deciding how many less important elements to eliminate randomly. Even though the elements might be eliminated in one loop, they still have chance to be recovered back during the random walk with the acceptance-rejection mechanism. Starting from a sparser network, an optimal sub-configuration can be determined iteratively by Simulated Annealing algorithm. If the total number of elements is larger, the corresponding Markov chain must be longer so that a wider space is explored. The longer length indicates that the initial temperature should be higher and the decreasing rate should be closer to 1 so that SA has more time to search for the whole space.

While the network performance will strictly go down as there are increasingly more parameters being erased, the optimal permutation can always be determined under the pruning percentage setup in advance. Different from (Rere et al., 2015) in which applied SA to update weights parameter directly, we implemented it to decide which edges should be kept connected and which should not be in each iteration. In order to change connections in a more convenient way, the actual objects that we are fine-tuning would be the mask matrices. The whole optimization process starts from a network trained using back propagation approach.

After getting the optimized weight parameters, we erase a small fraction of total weight parameters and start the permutation process. These two steps can both be fulfilled by manipulating the mask matrices. According to the pruning objective, which is minimizing the same loss function _L_ ( _·_ ) applied in Eq.(3) by fine-tuning network configuration, the objective function can be formulated as follows.

The objective function Eq.(3) optimizes the configuration under a fixed preset fraction of randomly pruned network. When the new configuration is finalized, the remaining weight parameters should be fine-tuned again to make sure the network is stabilized. During the pruning process, the parameters are not actually being erased. Instead, the numbers are masked with zeros. The difference between mask matrices and dropout operation is that the gradient conducted by back propagation cannot update the parameters being masked by zeros, but can still update the parameters in dropout case.

Therefore, the optimal _wi[∗]_[and] _[ b][∗] i_[are specifically indicating those remaining parameters.][In order to] evaluate the performance and the effectiveness of **Algorithm 1** in the field of network pruning, we implement it to a simple and shallow fully connected network illustrated in **Figure 1** which contains only 2 hidden layers.

In both cases of one-shot and gradual pruning strategies, it basically follows the same framework composed of Eq.(3, 4, 5). Once there are more percentage of elements being eliminated, both Eq.(4) and Eq.(5) will need to be executed again in order to keep getting the optimal configuration (permutation). The efficacy of Eq.(4) can be obviously found in the following one-shot pruning experiment and the importance of Eq.(5) can be observable by comparing the results between these 2 strategies.

Here, we would like to note a shortcoming of this algorithm whether be one-shot or gradual pruning. The algorithm optimizes the structure for a given set of weights which were trained for the unpruned network. The optimal weights for a new configuration is not the same with weights taken from the unpruned network. Ideally, the Algorithm should train the new structure weights at each new structure proposal, however this is avoided due to computational complexity increased by the length of the Metropolis loop. Although the network is trained finally when the metropolis loop has ended, of course the decisions that were made by Accept/Reject ratio are affected by the untrained weights. Our aim is to show that even for this suboptimal case improvements can be obtained motivating future research into structure optimization.

## 3.7 ONE-SHOT PRUNING

One-shot pruning is a direct and time-efficient approach to recompose a network. Once the optimal parameters _w[∗]_ and _b[∗]_ are obtained in Eq.(3) with back propagation, the pruning and permutation process can be executed. These operations can be considered as some interference to a stable network. The more changes to the configuration, the higher loss value would be computed from the new configuration. This trend can be clearly observed in different percentage of one-shot pruning. Since the pruning process is only executed once, the above equations, Eq.(3)(4) and (5), will only be executed once, where the details are listed in **Algorithm 2** . In order to make sure that the permutation process done in **Algorithm 1** can work properly, the pruning process is necessary because both connected and disconnected edges will exist in a network.

However, the efficiency of one-shot pruning comes at a cost, which could have been avoided by the gradual adaptation process. Its performance would significantly drop especially when there are increasingly more parameters being pruned. This phenomenon can be observed from the cases by taking both _MNIST_ and _FASHION_ datasets as _Ttrain_ for our network in figure 2.

## 3.8 GRADUAL PRUNING

Gradual pruning is widely known to be a better solution to trim a network rather than pruning at once. Instead of executing the above equations only once, gradual pruning executes them multiple times to gradually decrease the total number of network connections. By doing this, the change in new configuration will get more time to fine-tune the remaining weight parameters so that the new loss value will keep close to the original loss value. In the case of gradual pruning, a small fraction should be setup in advance. In this paper, we applied 2%, 5%, and 10% in total.

In the beginning of the **Algorithm 2** , the fully connected network should also be well trained. For the next steps being done iteratively, a small preset fraction of weight parameters would be masked out randomly so that the network will contain both connected and disconnected types of edges. After getting an optimal permutation, the network will further be trained for few more epochs to certify weight parameters still remain at stable state. By repeatedly mask out more and more weight parameters, a final sparse network can be obtained.

In order to observe the effectiveness of **Algorithm 2** , we also designed two baseline strategies. The first one is to randomly select weight parameters and the second one is to select the minimum _K_ amount of absolute weight parameters ( _minK_ ) inspired by deep compression (Han et al., 2016). When there are gradually less remaining parameters, the statistics of total weight parameters are changed slightly because of the fine-tuning process in Eq.(5). This step plays an important role to prevent the performance of the network from dropping significantly when high percentage of weight parameters are pruned. In addition, optimization with back propagation has strong adaptability regardless of the pruning strategy. In the case of _minK_ , more weights were updated into larger

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

According to the procedures of various pruning strategies introduced above, the remaining weight parameters and the corresponding configuration can be really different. In order to evaluate the performance of the pruned network at different sparsity levels, two datasets were applied to the experiments individually in this paper. The neural network applied in our experiment is a simple structure, containing only 2 hidden layers with 32 and 16 nodes respectively. The illustration has been shown in Figure 1. Simulated annealing algorithm has shown its capability to find out the optimal configuration under the situation that all parameters are fixed. To solve a more complex optimization problems, more random walk is guarenteed to increase the performance of the final result. The experiments under 0, 1, 5, 10, 20, 50, and 100 _MLL_ have all been included in this paper. However, better optimization results come at the cost of time complexity of **Algorithm 1** . According to our experiments, 50 _MLL_ has been seen to be good compromise to get both good performance and tolerable time consumption.

## 4.1 ONE-SHOT PRUNING

The disadvantage of one-shot pruning strategy is that the accuracy will significantly drop when there are too many edges being pruned at one time. The trend has been illustrated in _figure 2_ . Before achieving the threshold of 80% sparsity level, the remaining network can still be recovered by applying back propagation to Eq.(5). Both _figure 5_ and _figure 6_ have shown the changes of network performance from the time when the network is randomly pruned to the time when the configuration is permuted and fine-tuned again. As there are more edges being pruned, the performance gap between the fine-tuned network and the original fully connected network is getting larger.

With the help of SA, our experiments have shown that the weight parameters fine-tuning process in Eq.(5) can be omitted when the pruning percentage is not high. It would become much more convenient without training the network in a GPU and the pruning process could even be executed on edge devices. In the case of shallow network used in this paper, SA with 100 _MLL_ can almost

recover the performance of the network being pruned 80%. In addition, when the Metropolis loop length is big enough, the optimized configuration is guarenteed to outperform the other configuration obtained from _minK_ strategy, which is suggested in deep compression (Han et al., 2016). The same trend can be observed in both datasets.

## 4.2 GRADUAL PRUNING UNDER DIFFERENT MLL

In the case of gradual pruning, there is one more hyperparameter included in the experiment, which is the fixed percentage being used to erase edges iteratively. Accordingly, we get one more dimension to compare the experimental results. Similar to the success obtained in one-shot pruning strategy, Simulated Annealing algorithm also defeats the other two approaches in gradual pruning when the Metropolis loop length is large enough. The accuracy of the network at different time steps has been shown in _figure 7_ and _figure 8_ . The network strictly undergoes the optimization process of the three equations Eq.(3, 4, 5). The trends of network performance were the results after being permutated and trained to fine-tune weight parameters again. The trends indicate that the network being pruned more percentage at one time during each iteration will decrease the performance faster. In order to maintain the performance of a sparser network, gradual pruning has been experimentally proved to be an valid strategy.

In addition, the fine-tuning step is capable of pushing the performance of pruned network to a higher level, where the accuracy would be increased up to 1%. This trend is observable before the hidden layer is masked less than half. According to the _figure 7_ and _figure 8_ at different masked percentage for the hidden layer, SA with large metropolis loop length (50+ _MLL_ ) always outperforms the others. The small amount of accuracy increment and large _MLL_ are proved that they can slow down the decrease in network accuracy especially when majority of network edges are pruned. Unlike the attention mechanism applied in SqueezeNet (Hu et al., 2018) quantifying the importance factor directly to the training process optimization with back propagation, SA needs huge amount of time to locate the global optima by the Boltzmann accept reject mechanism. This is the main disadvantage of SA.

## 5 CONCLUSION

The most important observation of our work is that direct pruning does not necessarily lead to optimal network structures and changing the network structure can give us better results even in a weight setting which was not optimized for the new configurations. It has also been experimentally shown that the weight parameters with small magnitude are not certain to be the less important parameters. With the help of SA and back propagation to update weight parameters, a more lightweight configuration can be obtained without sacrificing its performance. The main shortcoming of the presented algorithm is that for each new structure proposal during SA metropolis loop, weight training using backpropagation was not performed. Obviously, the resulting search is suboptimal. Unfortunately doing weight training hand in hand with structure training turned out to be computational prohibitive and impossible for portable devices. However, the results obtained even with this suboptimal scheme show the potential of structure optimization and motivate for further research into network optimization. Speeding up SA so that full optimization can be carried is the objective of our current research.

The other advantage of our work is that the pruning and permuting processes with Simulated Annealing algorithm involves only forward pass. It indicates that RAM space contained in a computer can be hugely saved, and the pruning process can therefore be executed on a less powerful computer. In addition, our work has strong extensibility to other types of network pruning. In this paper, we took edges as the basic element to fine-tune the configuration, which generated a very long finite Markov chain. However, being an intelligent state space exploration method, the Simulated Annealing algorithm can be coded also for other types of basic elements, such as node and Convolution filter. By asking SA which filter to erase instead of which edge, the time complexity can be reduced to acceptable levels in the pruning process of complex network with the help of GPU.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes an interesting application of simulated annealing (SA) to network pruning, aiming to optimize network structure by searching over discrete configurations. It correctly identifies a relevant problem—suboptimal pruning outcomes from heuristic methods—and attempts a principled search via SA. The idea of using SA for configuration search rather than weight optimization is conceptually sound and aligns with prior work in combinatorial optimization of neural architectures.

Overview issues:

- No ablation on neighborhood structure or cooling schedule: The paper provides no ablation study to assess how the choice of neighborhood structure (e.g., single vs. multiple edge swaps) or cooling parameters (initial temperature, decay rate) affects pruning performance. Section 3.1 defines a conservative neighborhood but does not compare it to alternatives, nor does it justify the fixed values of T_init = 10 and η = 0.98 in Section 3.4. Without sensitivity analysis, the claims about SA’s effectiveness are confounded by arbitrary hyperparameter choices. This undermines reproducibility and limits insight into what aspects of the search strategy actually contribute to performance.

- Inadequate comparison with competitive pruning baselines: The baselines used—random pruning and minK magnitude pruning—are outdated and do not reflect current state-of-the-art. The paper fails to compare against modern structured pruning methods such as MetaPruning, HRank, or differentiable approaches like DARTS-based pruning. Given that the experiments are on small fully connected networks, the omission is particularly concerning: competitive methods would likely outperform SA given similar sparsity levels. The lack of comparison with recent work weakens the claim of advancement.

- Unrealistic evaluation of computational cost: While the paper claims SA is suitable for edge devices due to its reliance on forward passes only, it ignores the high computational burden of long Metropolis chains. The experiments use up to 100 MLL iterations, each involving many forward evaluations, which is infeasible on resource-limited devices. No runtime or FLOP count is reported, making the efficiency claims unsubstantiated. In contrast, magnitude-based pruning is nearly free and differentiable methods amortize search cost over training. The paper must quantify search cost to support its practicality claims.

- Lack of validation for the heuristic attention mechanism: Section 3.5 introduces a 'heuristic attention mechanism' equating SA’s discrete mask selection with attention, but provides no empirical evidence that the selected edges correspond to meaningful importance. Unlike Squeeze-and-Excitation or gradient-based saliency, the method does not correlate mask patterns with input features or downstream performance. The term 'attention' is misleading without validation, and the mechanism should instead be framed as a search strategy, not an interpretability tool.

- Overstatement of convergence and optimality claims: The paper repeatedly claims SA finds the 'optimal' or 'best' configuration (e.g., Section 1, 3.6), but this is only true in the limit of infinite time. In practice, the finite MLL and fixed cooling schedule mean convergence to global optima is unlikely, especially in high-dimensional spaces. Theoretical convergence (cited from van Laarhoven & Aarts) does not imply practical feasibility. The claims should be tempered to reflect that SA provides a heuristic search with no guarantee of optimality under the given budget.

- Limited scalability and generalization evidence: All experiments are conducted on a shallow two-layer fully connected network. There is no evaluation on convolutional networks, ResNets, or transformers—architectures where pruning is most relevant. The method’s extensibility to filter-level or block-level pruning is only speculated in the conclusion. Without results on standard benchmarks like CIFAR or ImageNet, the claims of broad applicability are unsupported. The paper should demonstrate at least one structured pruning variant on a CNN to establish relevance.

- No comparison with modern differentiable pruning methods: The paper claims advancement in network pruning via simulated annealing but compares only against random and magnitude-based baselines, omitting modern differentiable approaches like DARTS-based pruning or MetaPruning. Given that these methods are standard in structured pruning and operate under similar end-to-end training frameworks, their absence undermines the significance of the proposed method. To establish competitiveness, the authors should compare accuracy-sparsity trade-offs with at least one differentiable NAS-style pruning method on the same architecture and dataset. Without this, the claim of effectiveness remains unproven in the context of current practice.

- Missing computational cost metrics for edge deployment claims: The paper argues that SA is suitable for edge devices due to forward-pass-only execution, yet reports no runtime, memory footprint, or FLOP count for the search process. For a method using up to 100 Metropolis loop iterations, each involving multiple forward passes, the actual computational burden could easily exceed the capacity of mobile hardware. To support practicality claims, the authors must report average search time and memory usage per iteration on a representative device (e.g., Raspberry Pi or mobile GPU), and compare it to the negligible cost of magnitude pruning. Without such data, the efficiency argument is purely speculative.

- No validation on convolutional networks or standard benchmarks: All experiments are confined to a shallow fully connected network on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST, with no evaluation on CNNs or standard vision benchmarks like CIFAR-10. Since structured pruning is most relevant in convolutional architectures, this omission severely limits the paper's impact. The authors should apply their method to a small CNN (e.g., VGG-11 or ResNet-20) on CIFAR-10, using filter-level pruning with SA, and report accuracy under comparable sparsity levels. This would demonstrate feasibility beyond toy settings and align the evaluation with community standards.

- Unsubstantiated 'heuristic attention' interpretation: The paper introduces a 'heuristic attention mechanism' by equating SA’s mask selection with attention, but provides no evidence that the selected edges correlate with feature importance or input semantics. Unlike true attention mechanisms, there is no analysis showing that high-frequency connections in the final mask align with informative inputs or gradients. To justify the terminology, the authors should visualize top-preserved edges in a CNN experiment and compare them to gradient-based saliency maps or SE-block weights. Otherwise, the term should be dropped, and the mechanism described simply as a search heuristic.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Conduct ablation studies on neighborhood structure, MLL length, and cooling parameters with statistical reporting across runs

- Add comparisons with at least three modern pruning methods (e.g., magnitude-based with fine-tuning, HRank, DARTS-based pruning) on standard benchmarks

- Report computational cost (search time, FLOPs, memory) and compare with baseline methods to substantiate efficiency claims

- Remove or rigorously validate the 'heuristic attention mechanism' framing with saliency or ablation analysis

- Extend experiments to at least one convolutional network on CIFAR-10 and report accuracy-sparsity trade-offs

comments.title: Overstated optimality claims under finite computational budget
comments.quote: The optimized network is guaranteed to be the best permutated configuration under the fixed parameters.
comments.explanation: The claim of a 'guaranteed' optimal configuration is unjustified given the finite Metropolis loop length and fixed cooling schedule. Simulated annealing only converges to the global optimum in the limit of infinite time; with practical constraints like 100 MLL iterations and geometric cooling, such guarantees do not hold. This creates a misleading impression of deterministic optimality. The statement should be revised to reflect that SA heuristically searches for high-quality configurations, with theoretical convergence under idealized conditions.

comments.title: Incorrect loss difference expression with undefined negated mask
comments.quote: At every move, $-\Delta\mathcal{L}(\cdot)=\mathcal{L}(M)-\mathcal{L}(-M^{\prime})$ is calculated, if it is larger than $0$, the new state is accepted, otherwise a uniform number is generated between $[0,1)$ and compared with $\mathcal{P}_{b}$. If $\mathcal{P}_{b}$ greater it is accepted, otherwise it is rejected.
comments.explanation: The expression $\mathcal{L}(-M^{\prime})$ is undefined—negating a binary mask yields values in \{-1,0\}, which does not correspond to a valid network configuration. The intended quantity is almost certainly $\mathcal{L}(M')$, making this a critical notational error. Additionally, the acceptance logic is malformed: it fails to specify comparison with a uniform random variable $u \sim \text{Uniform}[0,1)$. Without this, the algorithm reduces to deterministic acceptance when $\mathcal{P}_b > 0$, violating the stochastic nature of SA. The text must be corrected to define $\Delta\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}(M') - \mathcal{L}(M)$ and use $u < \mathcal{P}_b$ for acceptance.

comments.title: Misleading claim about forward-pass-only execution
comments.quote: The other advantage of our work is that the pruning and permuting processes with Simulated Annealing algorithm involves only forward pass. It indicates that RAM space contained in a computer can be hugely saved, and the pruning process can therefore be executed on a less powerful computer.
comments.explanation: This claim is factually incorrect. The method explicitly includes backpropagation for weight fine-tuning (Eq. 5) after structural changes, which requires full backward passes and substantial memory. Forward passes alone cannot update weights. Therefore, the assertion of 'only forward pass' misrepresents the computational demands and falsely implies memory savings that do not exist. The statement should be removed or revised to acknowledge that while structure proposals rely on forward evaluation, full retraining requires gradient computation and negates the claimed RAM advantage.

comments.title: False equivalence between SA and attention mechanisms
comments.quote: Being a new incorporation between SA and network pruning, we call our method as the heuristic attention mechanism.
comments.explanation: Labeling the pruning mask selection as an 'attention mechanism' is misleading. True attention dynamically reweights features based on input-dependent importance via learned parameters. Here, the mask is a static, discrete structure determined by stochastic search, not a differentiable, input-adaptive module. The binary \{0,1\} mask does not modulate features during inference. This terminology conflates structural pruning with attention, potentially confusing readers about the method's function. The term should be dropped in favor of 'pruning mask optimization via simulated annealing' or similar.

comments.title: Unjustified convergence claim requiring per-loop stationarity
comments.quote: The third parameter $\mathcal{MLL}$ should be large enough for the Markov chain to converge to the stationary distribution and small enough not lose time unnecessarily beyond convergence.
comments.explanation: This statement incorrectly assumes that each Metropolis loop must reach stationarity—a requirement not supported by SA theory. In practice, SA functions effectively even with short chains at each temperature, relying on slow cooling and long overall runtime. Requiring convergence within each loop would make the algorithm prohibitively slow and contradicts standard implementations. The cited literature does not impose this condition. Replace 'converge to the stationary distribution' with 'allow adequate exploration at each temperature' to align with actual SA behavior.

comments.title: Misattribution of 'tight compression' to Chen et al.
comments.quote: In order to truely eliminate the parameters from the network, *(Chen et al., 2020)* proposed tight compression which converts the resulting large sparse matrix after pruning to a smaller but dense matrix by moving non-zero members to matrix locations with zeros. These moves are not done in a deterministic manner but instead via stochastic heuristic algorithm namely simulated annealing.
comments.explanation: This attribution is incorrect. No such method called 'tight compression' appears in Chen et al. (2020), nor is simulated annealing used to rearrange non-zero elements into dense storage. Standard sparse-to-dense conversion (e.g., CSR) is deterministic and unrelated to SA. The authors appear to conflate weight pruning with memory layout optimization—a non-standard and unjustified use of SA. The citation must be removed, and the text revised to clarify that SA is applied to pruning mask selection, not matrix compaction.

comments.title: Contradictory efficiency claims due to fine-tuning reliance
comments.quote: In addition, the fine-tuning step is capable of pushing the performance of pruned network to a higher level, where the accuracy would be increased up to 1%.
comments.explanation: This emphasis on fine-tuning undermines the paper's central efficiency argument. While fine-tuning improves accuracy, it requires full backpropagation and retraining—operations that are memory-intensive and incompatible with edge deployment. The paper promotes SA as suitable for low-resource devices due to forward-pass-only execution, yet relies on computationally expensive fine-tuning for performance. Either the role of fine-tuning must be downplayed, or the efficiency claims revised. Presenting fine-tuning as essential contradicts the lightweight narrative.

comments.title: Incorrect neighborhood move preserving row sparsity
comments.quote: The neighbouring states are defined to be new configurations obtained by moving one (or more) branch(es) originally at location $(i,j)$ to $(i,k)$. Equivalently, can be visualised as the move of one $1$ from matrix location $(i,j)$ to another position on the same row $(i,k)$.
comments.explanation: This definition implies that connections are relocated rather than added or removed, preserving the number of outgoing edges per node. Such a constraint forces fixed row-wise sparsity, which is not standard in pruning. A correct neighborhood should allow flipping individual mask bits (add/remove edges) to enable flexible sparsity patterns. The current formulation artificially restricts the search space and does not reflect typical unstructured or structured pruning goals. Revise to define neighbors as configurations differing by a single edge addition or removal.

comments.title: Unsupported claim of SA 'defeating' baselines without statistical evidence
comments.quote: Simulated Annealing algorithm also defeats the other two approaches in gradual pruning when the Metropolis loop length is large enough.
comments.explanation: The term 'defeats' implies consistent, statistically significant superiority, but no error bars, variance, or significance tests are reported. Results are based on the mean of five runs, yet without uncertainty measures, observed differences could be due to random variation. For example, if the accuracy gap is within the standard deviation, the claim is unsubstantiated. Replace 'defeats' with 'tends to outperform' and ensure figures include confidence intervals to support comparative claims.

comments.title: Ambiguous and undefined $\mathcal{MLL}$ notation
comments.quote: The experiments under 0, 1, 5, 10, 20, 50, and  $100\mathcal{MLL}$  have all been included in this paper. However, better optimization results come at the cost of time complexity of Algorithm 1. According to our experiments,  $50\mathcal{MLL}$  has been seen to be good compromise to get both good performance and tolerable time consumption.
comments.explanation: The term $\mathcal{MLL}$ is used without definition. While context suggests it means 'Metropolis Loop Length' or number of iterations, this is never confirmed. The notation $100\mathcal{MLL}$ implies a unit or scaling factor, creating ambiguity about whether it refers to 100 iterations or some normalized budget. This undermines reproducibility and clarity. Define $\mathcal{MLL}$ explicitly at first use—for example, as 'number of Metropolis loop iterations'—and avoid treating it as a multiplicative unit.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of joint training feasibility on edge devices
comments.quote: Unfortunately doing weight training hand in hand with structure training turned out to be computational prohibitive and impossible for portable devices.
comments.explanation: The term 'impossible' overstates the limitation. While joint training is computationally expensive, it is not categorically infeasible on portable devices—methods like progressive pruning, quantization-aware training, and low-rank updates are routinely deployed on edge hardware. The paper’s own algorithm already uses backpropagation, so the infrastructure exists. The issue is one of efficiency, not possibility. Revise to 'currently impractical under our implementation' to accurately reflect the practical bottleneck without overstating hardware constraints.

comments.title: Incorrect implication that SA rearranges matrix elements
comments.quote: These moves are not done in a deterministic manner but instead via stochastic heuristic algorithm namely simulated annealing.
comments.explanation: The sentence suggests that simulated annealing physically rearranges non-zero elements within a matrix during compression. This misrepresents the method: SA optimizes the pruning mask—i.e., which weights to keep—not the memory layout of the remaining values. The algorithm searches over discrete configurations, not matrix index permutations. There is no evidence in the literature that SA is used for runtime matrix compaction in neural networks. Clarify that SA selects high-performing sparsity patterns, not memory reallocation strategies.

comments.title: Sparsity level ambiguity in 'pruned 80%' phrasing
comments.quote: recover the performance of the network being pruned 80%.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'pruned 80%' is ambiguous—it could mean 80% of weights removed (80% sparsity) or 80% retained (20% sparsity). Given prior mention of '80% sparsity level', the intended meaning is likely the former, but the syntax allows misreading. In a quantitative context, such imprecision undermines clarity. Rewrite as 'pruned to 80% sparsity' or 'with 80% of edges removed' to eliminate ambiguity.

comments.title: Misuse of 'permutated' to describe retraining process
comments.quote: The trends of network performance were the results after being permutated and trained to fine-tune weight parameters again.
comments.explanation: The term 'permutated' is incorrect here. Permutation refers to reordering elements, but the context describes retraining after pruning, not structural rearrangement. The intended meaning is that the network was updated or retrained. Using 'permutated' introduces confusion about the nature of the fine-tuning process. Replace with 'retrained' or 'updated' to accurately reflect the procedure.

comments.title: Spelling and grammatical errors in key terms
comments.quote:
It is known that the convergence is guaranteed in the case of logaritmic cooling, however this requires infinitely slow cooling. Instead, most users prefer geometric cooling

$T_{n+1}=\eta T_{n}.$

The important parameters to be set are the initial temperature ($T_{init}$), the cooling rate ($\eta$) between Metrolopis loops, and the Metropolis loop length ($\mathcal{MLL}$).
comments.explanation: The words 'logaritmic' and 'Metrolopis' are misspelled; they should be 'logarithmic' and 'Metropolis', respectively. 'Metropolis' refers to the well-known Metropolis-Hastings algorithm and must be spelled correctly. These errors reduce professionalism and could confuse readers unfamiliar with the terminology. Correct all instances to ensure clarity and credibility.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes using simulated annealing (SA) for network pruning optimization, claiming it can find globally optimal pruned network structures. While the core idea of using heuristic optimization for structure search is interesting, the paper suffers from significant methodological limitations including inadequate experimental validation, unsupported theoretical claims, and incomplete analysis of computational feasibility.

The paper's main contribution is framing network pruning as a combinatorial optimization problem solvable by simulated annealing. The authors correctly identify that standard pruning methods don't optimize for structural optimality. However, the experimental evaluation is limited to a tiny 2-layer network on simple datasets, the theoretical convergence claims don't match the practical implementation, and key components like the 'heuristic attention mechanism' are poorly defined.

Overview issues:

- Theoretical convergence claims contradict implementation: The paper claims SA can find the global minimum for this NP-hard problem (Section 3), but the actual implementation uses geometric cooling (η=0.98) which does not guarantee convergence to global optima. Only logarithmic cooling provides this guarantee, but the authors dismiss it as requiring 'infinitely slow cooling' (Section 3.4). More fundamentally, the authors acknowledge they don't retrain weights during SA proposals due to computational cost (Section 3.6), meaning the search operates on suboptimal weight configurations. This creates a fundamental disconnect between the theoretical claims of finding global optima and the actual suboptimal search procedure.

- Insufficient experimental validation for generalizability claims: The experiments are limited to a shallow 2-layer fully connected network (32 and 16 hidden units) on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST (Section 4, Figure 1). The paper claims extensibility to 'other types of network pruning' including CNNs and filters (Section 5), but provides no evidence. Modern pruning research typically evaluates on ImageNet-scale models like ResNets or MobileNets. Without testing on deeper networks, convolutional architectures, or larger datasets, the claims about practical applicability remain unsubstantiated. The tiny test network also makes it impossible to assess whether SA scales to realistic pruning problems.

- Missing computational efficiency analysis: The paper claims the method can run on edge devices since it only requires forward passes (Section 5), but provides no actual runtime measurements or memory usage analysis. The Metropolis loop lengths tested (up to 100 MLL) suggest significant computational overhead. There's no comparison of total pruning time against baseline methods, nor analysis of how runtime scales with network size or sparsity level. Without concrete efficiency metrics, the claims about practical deployment are unsupported. The trade-off between optimization quality and computational cost remains unquantified.

- Inadequate baseline comparisons: The method is only compared against random pruning and magnitude-based minK pruning (Section 3.8). Missing are comparisons with state-of-the-art pruning methods mentioned in the related work, such as structured pruning approaches (Li et al., 2017), lottery ticket methods (Frankle & Carbin, 2019), or other optimization-based pruning. The literature context identifies several relevant competitors that should be included for a proper evaluation. Without these comparisons, it's impossible to assess whether the proposed SA approach actually advances the state of the art.

- Heuristic attention mechanism poorly defined and evaluated: Section 3.5 introduces a 'heuristic attention mechanism' but provides no clear definition or evaluation. It's described as finding important elements via trial-and-error with Boltzmann probability, producing discrete {0,1} values for the mask matrix. However, this appears to be simply the standard SA process applied to pruning, not a distinct mechanism. No ablation study shows what value this adds beyond basic SA. The comparison to SqueezeNet's attention mechanism is confusing since that approach uses learned continuous weights while this uses binary masks determined by SA.

- Hyperparameter selection lacks justification: Key SA hyperparameters (T_init=10, η=0.98) are chosen without justification (Section 3.4). No sensitivity analysis shows how performance varies with these choices. The Metropolis loop length is tested from 0-100 MLL, but 50 is selected as a 'good compromise' without systematic evaluation. Different neighborhood structures (single vs multiple edge moves) are mentioned but not compared. The cooling scheme's impact on convergence rate and solution quality is not analyzed, making it difficult to reproduce or optimize the method.

- Missing demonstration of structural optimality: The paper claims to optimize network structure but never demonstrates that the SA-found configuration is actually better than alternatives beyond the two simple baselines. To show the method has bite, the authors should include a concrete example where a naive pruning approach (like magnitude-based pruning) leads to a clearly suboptimal structure, while SA finds a better-performing configuration with the same sparsity level. This could be visualized by showing two networks with identical parameter counts but different connectivity patterns, where the SA-optimized network achieves higher accuracy. Such a demonstration would prove that structure optimization, not just parameter removal, drives the improvement.

- No worked example for CNN filter pruning: The paper claims extensibility to convolutional filter pruning but provides no concrete example. A complete paper should include at least one worked computation showing how the SA state space and neighborhood structure would be defined for a simple CNN (e.g., pruning filters in a single conv layer of ResNet-18). The authors should specify how the mask matrix would represent filter presence/absence, define the neighborhood moves (which filters can be swapped), and compute the acceptance probability for a hypothetical move. This would demonstrate that the method actually generalizes beyond the toy fully-connected network.

- Missing practical implementation guidance: The paper claims edge device deployment but provides no implementation details. Readers need guidance on how to implement the forward-pass-only SA pruning in practice. The authors should include pseudocode showing how to efficiently compute loss differences without backpropagation, discuss memory requirements for storing intermediate states, and provide runtime estimates for different network sizes. Without this, the claim that the method 'can be executed on less powerful computers' remains unsubstantiated.

- Underdeveloped gradual pruning analysis: The paper introduces gradual pruning but fails to analyze its theoretical advantages. The authors claim gradual pruning is better but don't explain why mathematically. They should include an analysis comparing the search space exploration between one-shot and gradual approaches, perhaps showing how gradual pruning allows better local exploration around intermediate optima. A simple example demonstrating how a sequence of small moves can reach a configuration inaccessible to one large jump would strengthen this section and justify the additional computational cost.

Recommendation: Major revision. The paper presents an interesting idea but suffers from significant methodological gaps. The theoretical framework doesn't align with the implementation, experiments are insufficient for the claims made, and key components lack proper evaluation. Substantial work is needed to validate the approach on realistic networks and provide rigorous analysis.

Revision targets:

- Add experiments on modern architectures (CNNs, possibly Transformers) with larger datasets to demonstrate generalizability beyond tiny FC networks

- Provide computational efficiency analysis including runtime, memory usage, and scalability measurements to support edge deployment claims

- Include comprehensive baseline comparisons with state-of-the-art pruning methods from the literature

- Clarify or remove the 'heuristic attention mechanism' concept, and if kept, provide ablation studies showing its distinct contribution

- Add hyperparameter sensitivity analysis and justification for SA parameter choices

comments.title: Theoretical claim about global optimum lacks justification
comments.quote: We propose to do network optimization by Simulated Annealing, a heuristic based non-convex optimization method which can potentially solve this NP-hard problem and find the global minimum for a given percentage of branch pruning given sufficient amount of time.
comments.explanation:
The claim that simulated annealing can find the global minimum for an NP-hard problem is misleading. While SA can theoretically converge to the global optimum with logarithmic cooling schedules, the paper later admits to using geometric cooling (η=0.98) which does not provide this guarantee. The statement 'given sufficient amount of time' is also problematic - for NP-hard problems, 'sufficient time' could be exponential. The abstract should be corrected to reflect that SA finds high-quality local optima or near-optimal solutions, not guaranteed global minima.

Rewrite the sentence as: 'We propose to do network optimization by Simulated Annealing, a heuristic based non-convex optimization method which can find high-quality solutions for this NP-hard problem and approach the global minimum for a given percentage of branch pruning with appropriate cooling schedules.'

comments.title: Incorrect claim about existing pruning approaches
comments.quote: The existing pruning approaches do not consider the optimality of the resulting network structure in any means.
comments.explanation: This claim is factually incorrect and contradicts the literature context provided. The paper's own literature review mentions several approaches that explicitly consider structural optimality, including ABC optimization for channel-wise pruning (Lin et al., 2020b) and GAN-based parameter selection using feature map similarity (Madaan et al., 2020). Both methods evaluate the quality of the resulting network structure. The authors should qualify this statement to specify which aspects of optimality existing approaches miss, rather than making an absolute claim. Rewrite as: "Many existing pruning approaches focus primarily on weight magnitude criteria and may not fully optimize the global network structure under sparsity constraints."

comments.title: Unsubstantiated optimality guarantee claim
comments.quote: The optimized network is guaranteed to be the best permutated configuration under the fixed parameters.
comments.explanation: This claim overstates what simulated annealing can guarantee. SA provides asymptotic convergence to global optima only under specific conditions (like logarithmic cooling schedules), but the paper later admits using geometric cooling which doesn't provide this guarantee. The statement is also problematic because SA is a heuristic that doesn't guarantee finding the global optimum in finite time. The authors should remove the guarantee language and instead state that SA "aims to find high-quality configurations" or "can explore the solution space effectively." This aligns with both theoretical limitations of SA and the paper's actual implementation.

comments.title: Incorrect attribution of tight compression method
comments.quote: In order to truely eliminate the parameters from the network, *(Chen et al., 2020)* proposed tight compression which converts the resulting large sparse matrix after pruning to a smaller but dense matrix by moving non-zero members to matrix locations with zeros.
comments.explanation: The attribution of tight compression to Chen et al. (2020) appears incorrect. The method described - converting sparse matrices to smaller dense matrices by moving non-zero elements - is actually the core contribution of Han et al. (2016) Deep Compression paper, specifically their "weight rearrangement" or "matrix compaction" step. Chen et al. (2020) is not the correct reference for this technique. This misattribution matters because it misrepresents the literature and the paper's relationship to prior work. Rewrite the sentence as: "In order to truly eliminate the parameters from the network, Han et al. (2016) proposed weight rearrangement which converts the resulting large sparse matrix after pruning to a smaller but dense matrix by moving non-zero members to matrix locations with zeros."

comments.title: Neighborhood definition violates sparsity constraint
comments.quote: The neighbouring states are defined to be new configurations obtained by moving one (or more) branch(es) originally at location $(i,j)$ to $(i,k)$. Equivalently, can be visualised as the move of one $1$ from matrix location $(i,j)$ to another position on the same row $(i,k)$. The choice of a single branch replacement corresponds to a conservative neighbourhood structure. It can be made more liberal by considering more than one replacements.
comments.explanation: The neighborhood definition as 'moving' a branch from $(i,j)$ to $(i,k)$ is mathematically inconsistent with maintaining a fixed sparsity level. Moving a 1 from position $(i,j)$ to $(i,k)$ would require setting $M_{l}(i,j)=0$ and $M_{l}(i,k)=1$, but this only preserves the total number of connections if $M_{l}(i,k)$ was originally 0. However, the definition doesn't specify this condition, and if $M_{l}(i,k)=1$ initially, this move would increase the total number of connections, violating the pruning constraint. Rewrite the quoted text as 'The neighbouring states are defined to be new configurations obtained by swapping one (or more) branch(es) originally at location $(i,j)$ with empty positions $(i,k)$ where $M_{l}(i,k)=0$. Equivalently, can be visualised as the move of one $1$ from matrix location $(i,j)$ to another position on the same row $(i,k)$ that was previously 0.' because this ensures constant sparsity.

comments.title: Incorrect loss difference sign in acceptance probability
comments.quote: At every move, $-\Delta\mathcal{L}(\cdot)=\mathcal{L}(M)-\mathcal{L}(-M^{\prime})$ is calculated, if it is larger than $0$, the new state is accepted, otherwise a uniform number is generated between $[0,1)$ and compared with $\mathcal{P}_{b}$. If $\mathcal{P}_{b}$ greater it is accepted, otherwise it is rejected.
comments.explanation: The acceptance probability formula and loss difference definition are inconsistent. The paper defines $-\Delta\mathcal{L}(\cdot)=\mathcal{L}(M)-\mathcal{L}(M')$ and uses it in $\mathcal{P}_{b}=\min(1,\exp(-\Delta\mathcal{L}(\cdot)/(kT)))$. Substituting gives $\mathcal{P}_{b}=\min(1,\exp((\mathcal{L}(M)-\mathcal{L}(M'))/(kT)))$, which is incorrect for minimization. The standard Boltzmann acceptance should be $\mathcal{P}_{b}=\min(1,\exp(-(\mathcal{L}(M')-\mathcal{L}(M))/(kT)))$. Rewrite the acceptance probability as $\mathcal{P}_{b}=\min\left(1,\exp\left(\frac{\mathcal{L}(M)-\mathcal{L}(M')}{k\cdot T}\right)\right)$ to match the acceptance logic, or define $\Delta\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}(M')-\mathcal{L}(M)$ and keep the original formula.

comments.title: Incorrect convergence claim for Simulated Annealing
comments.quote: It can be shown that the accept/reject mechanism using the Boltzmann function in Equation (1) ensures that the detailed balance condition and therefore converges to the unique stationary distribution *(van Laarhoven & Aarts, 1987)*. This property is shared with the Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithm which aims to obtain the posterior distribution of model parameters. Both algorithms construct Metropolis loops via random walks over a Markov chains satisfying the detailed balance condition. The difference is that Simulated Annealing algorithm employs several Metropolis loops with decreasing temperature parameter $T$ in Equation 1. Hence, posterior distribution approximated by every Metropolis loop gets more and more peaked at the maximum of the stationary distribution. In this way, the parameter values that maximize the stationary distribution of the Markov chains is obtained.
comments.explanation: The claim that Simulated Annealing converges to the unique stationary distribution is incorrect. While each Metropolis loop at fixed temperature satisfies detailed balance and converges to its stationary distribution, Simulated Annealing uses a decreasing temperature schedule, which breaks the time-homogeneity required for convergence to a unique stationary distribution. The algorithm is designed to converge to the global optimum (not a stationary distribution) under specific cooling schedules (typically logarithmic). Rewrite as: 'Each Metropolis loop at fixed temperature satisfies detailed balance and converges to its stationary distribution. Simulated Annealing uses a sequence of such loops with decreasing temperature, which under appropriate cooling schedules can converge to the global optimum of the objective function.'

comments.title: Convergence claim contradicts implementation
comments.quote:
It is known that the convergence is guaranteed in the case of logaritmic cooling, however this requires infinitely slow cooling. Instead, most users prefer geometric cooling

$T_{n+1}=\eta T_{n}.$

The important parameters to be set are the initial temperature ($T_{init}$), the cooling rate ($\eta$) between Metrolopis loops, and the Metropolis loop length ($\mathcal{MLL}$).
comments.explanation: The first-pass reviewer correctly identifies a theoretical inconsistency. The paper states that logarithmic cooling guarantees convergence but requires infinitely slow cooling, then switches to geometric cooling without addressing convergence implications. Geometric cooling with η=0.98 indeed does not guarantee convergence to the global optimum - it can get trapped in local minima. The paper should explicitly acknowledge this trade-off: geometric cooling sacrifices theoretical convergence guarantees for practical speed. Add a sentence after the geometric cooling equation stating: 'While geometric cooling with η=0.98 sacrifices the theoretical convergence guarantees of logarithmic cooling, it provides a practical balance between exploration and computational efficiency for our neural network pruning application.'

comments.title: Contradictory description of equation (3)'s purpose
comments.quote: The objective function Eq.(3) optimizes the configuration under a fixed preset fraction of randomly pruned network.
comments.explanation: The statement is indeed contradictory. Eq.(3) is $\min_{w_i^*, b_i^*} \mathcal{L}(N(\mathcal{T}_{train}, |\{w_i, b_i\}_{i=1}^k, \mathcal{T}_{train}^{labels}))$, which optimizes weight parameters with no mask matrices or configuration variables. The text describes configuration optimization but the equation shows weight optimization. Rewrite as 'The objective function Eq.(3) optimizes the weight parameters for the unpruned network, while Eq.(4) optimizes the mask configuration for fixed weights under a fixed preset fraction of randomly pruned network' because the current description mischaracterizes Eq.(3)'s mathematical content.

comments.title: Circular reasoning about pruning necessity
comments.quote: In order to make sure that the permutation process done in Algorithm 1 can work properly, the pruning process is necessary because both connected and disconnected edges will exist in a network.
comments.explanation:
The reasoning is indeed circular. The text claims pruning is necessary for permutation to work because both connected and disconnected edges will exist, but the disconnected edges only exist because of pruning itself. Without pruning, the trained network would have all edges connected by definition. The permutation process should logically work on either a fully connected or partially connected network, so pruning cannot be a prerequisite for permutation to function properly.

Rewrite the sentence as: "The permutation process in Algorithm 1 operates on the network structure after pruning, rearranging the remaining connections while preserving the overall connectivity pattern established by the pruning decisions."

comments.title: Unsubstantiated guarantee claim
comments.quote: In addition, when the Metropolis loop length is big enough, the optimized configuration is guarenteed to outperform the other configuration obtained from $min\mathcal{K}$ strategy, which is suggested in deep compression *(Han et al., 2016)*.
comments.explanation: The claim that SA is 'guarenteed' to outperform minK strategy when Metropolis loop length is 'big enough' is mathematically incorrect. Simulated annealing provides probabilistic guarantees of convergence to global optima under specific conditions (like logarithmic cooling), but never guarantees outperforming a specific deterministic method like minK. The paper uses geometric cooling (η=0.98) which doesn't even provide global convergence guarantees. Remove the word 'guarenteed' and replace with 'tends to' or 'in our experiments consistently' to reflect the empirical nature of this observation.

comments.title: Contradiction about weight optimization
comments.quote: With the help of SA and back propagation to update weight parameters, a more lightweight configuration can be obtained without sacrificing its performance. The main shortcoming of the presented algorithm is that for each new structure proposal during SA metropolis loop, weight training using backpropagation was not performed.
comments.explanation:
The first sentence claims that SA and backpropagation work together to update weight parameters, but the second sentence directly contradicts this by stating that backpropagation was not performed during the SA Metropolis loop. According to Section 3.6, the authors explicitly acknowledge they don't retrain weights during SA proposals due to computational cost. This creates a factual inconsistency about whether backpropagation is actually used in the algorithm.

Rewrite the first sentence as "With the help of SA to explore network structures, a more lightweight configuration can be obtained without sacrificing its performance, even though weight parameters are not retrained during the structure search." because the algorithm does not use backpropagation during SA proposals.

comments.title: Overgeneralization from fully connected networks
comments.quote: Our results have shown that Simulated Annealing can significantly reduce the complexity of a fully connected neural network with only limited loss of performance.
comments.explanation:
This statement is technically accurate but misleading given the paper's broader claims. The abstract suggests general network pruning capabilities, but the actual results are limited to a shallow 2-layer fully connected network on simple datasets. The claim should be qualified to reflect this limitation, as readers might assume the method has been validated on more complex architectures.
